title: Source lineage for the Middle English Glossed Prose Psalter (baa8159)
summary: The repository artifact is a pinned, structurally normalized copy of U-M BAA8159.0001.001. U-M OCR was made from a complete 220-image facsimile of Bülbring's 1891 edition. Bülbring printed the English text of BL Add MS 17376, using Matthias Konrath's careful working transcript for Psalms 1-37 and his own London transcription thereafter, and collated TCD MS 69 throughout. Four complete English witnesses now are known. A later BnF French witness represents the intermediary French tradition but is not the translator's physical exemplar. Complete raw U-M, Princeton, and Gallica bundles and complete Bülbring and Charzyńska PDFs are cached; TCD's viewer was access-challenged, BL has a paid photography route, and Pepys 2498 is absent from the current official selection.

entities.label: Middle English Glossed Prose Psalter
entities.description: Anonymous Middle English prose translation of 150 Psalms, eleven canticles, and the Athanasian Creed, at least partly mediated through French from a glossed Gallican Psalter.
notes: This is a conceptual work/source entity represented as catalog_description because the lineage schema has no intellectual-work or conceptual-source entity type; it is not a physical carrier or facsimile.

entities.label: Unidentified glossed Gallican Psalter source
entities.description: The Latin Gallican Psalter reshaped by extensive unidentified Latin glosses, attributed in the Pepys prologue to a Gregory who has not been identified.
notes: This is a conceptual work/source entity represented as catalog_description because the lineage schema has no intellectual-work or conceptual-source entity type; it is not a physical carrier or facsimile.

entities.label: Lost or unidentified French Psalter intermediary
entities.description: A medieval French translation of the same glossed Latin Psalter, used at least in part by the Middle English translator; its physical exemplar is unknown.
notes: This is a conceptual work/source entity represented as catalog_description because the lineage schema has no intellectual-work or conceptual-source entity type; it is not a physical carrier or facsimile.

entities.label: Lost Middle English Glossed Prose Psalter archetype
entities.description: A conceptual lost archetype required by Black and St-Jacques's stemmatic account; Pepys probably derives independently from it, but Pepys is not itself the original.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 17376
entities.description: A fourteenth-century vellum octavo composite. The current British Library record describes a Latin/English Psalter with canticles, the Athanasian Creed, prayers, and related matter in the opening portion, followed from f. 150 by Middle English religious poems and an imperfect doctrinal poem beginning at f. 206. Hanna identifies nine Latin leaves formerly at the front of part I as now separately preserved as Bodleian MS Lat. th. e. 32. Directly inspected historical sources report 220 or 221 leaves.

physical_description.extent: Current catalog: 1 item; Konrath reports 220 leaves and Kenyon 221

date_statements.label: Hanna's part-layer proposals
date_statements.value: c.1330-70 for part I; possibly c.1330-48 for poem-bearing part II

notes: The 220/221-leaf disagreement is retained at source level; the current BL record does not supply a leaf count.
The detached Bodleian leaves are recorded as a separate current physical entity.

This reusable entity core contains only intrinsic identity, holding, physical, date, and contents facts; work-specific loci and exclusions belong in scoped relations, access records, evidence, and questions.

entities.label: Trinity College Dublin MS 69
entities.description: Composite manuscript of 124 leaves, cataloged as IE TCD MS 69 (formerly A.4.4 and H.32), containing a Middle English prose Psalter and other components.

entities.label: Princeton, Scheide Library, MS 143
entities.description: Parchment codex of 61 leaves written in southern England in the second half of the fourteenth century.

date_statements.label: second half of the fourteenth century

entities.label: Cambridge, Magdalene College, Pepys MS 2498
entities.description: Parchment manuscript of about 1400, paginated rather than foliated.

entities.label: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Français 6260
entities.description: Fifteenth-century French parchment manuscript of 242 leaves containing Histoire de la Bible, Psautier et Apocalypse.
notes: Collateral French representative, not an English witness and not the translator's physical exemplar.

entities.label: Karl D. Bülbring, The Earliest Complete English Prose Psalter (1891)
entities.description: EETS Original Series 97, Part I: Preface and Text. Prints the complete English target on pp.1-196 with London-manuscript notes and Dublin variants. The promised Introduction, Notes, and Glossary were not published.

bibliographic.citation: Karl D. Bülbring, ed., The Earliest Complete English Prose Psalter, together with Eleven Canticles and a Translation of the Athanasian Creed, Part I: Preface and Text, EETS Original Series 97 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1891).
bibliographic.title: The Earliest Complete English Prose Psalter, together with Eleven Canticles and a Translation of the Athanasian Creed, Part I, Preface and Text
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 97

entities.label: Matthias Konrath's working transcript of the first thirty-seven Psalms
entities.description: A careful scholarly copy of Psalms 1-37 from the London manuscript, presented by Konrath to Bülbring before publication. Current location, physical form, and survival are unknown.
notes: This is a modern handwritten working transcript represented as manuscript_witness, not a medieval witness.

entities.label: Robert Ray Black and Raymond St-Jacques, The Middle English Glossed Prose Psalter (2012)
entities.description: Two-volume edition based on Pepys 2498, with variants from the other three English witnesses, the glossed Latin, all of the French representative in BnF Français 6260, introduction, commentary, appendices, glossary, and bibliography.

bibliographic.citation: Robert Ray Black and Raymond St-Jacques, eds., The Middle English Glossed Prose Psalter, 2 vols., Middle English Texts 45–46 (Heidelberg: Universitätsverlag Winter, 2012).
bibliographic.title: The Middle English Glossed Prose Psalter

entities.label: Magdalena Charzyńska-Wójcik, Text and Context in Jerome's Psalters (2013)
entities.description: Scholarly monograph whose section 2.3 synthesizes title, date, witnesses, authorship, French mediation, and gloss-source research for the target.
notes: This scholarly study is represented as catalog_description because the schema has no scholarly-study entity type.

editorial_practices.source_feature: London main text with manuscript and Dublin apparatus
editorial_practices.representation: Bülbring printed the English of L (BL Add MS 17376) as main text. Immediate notes identify L's corruptions and later corrections; a lower apparatus gives substantive D (TCD MS 69) variants while usually omitting spelling and dialect differences.
editorial_practices.description: Bülbring printed the English of L (BL Add MS 17376) as main text. Immediate notes identify L's corruptions and later corrections; a lower apparatus gives substantive D (TCD MS 69) variants while usually omitting spelling and dialect differences.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Selective correction of gross or senseless London readings
editorial_practices.representation: Bülbring says he did not correct every error of L, restricting intervention to the grossest, ridiculous, and senseless readings. The second-part Notes that would have discussed remaining problems were never published.
editorial_practices.description: Bülbring says he did not correct every error of L, restricting intervention to the grossest, ridiculous, and senseless readings. The second-part Notes that would have discussed remaining problems were never published.

editorial_practices.source_feature: English main text without complete interlinear Latin
editorial_practices.representation: The witnesses alternate Latin verse/gloss and English translation, but Bülbring's Part I prints the English as main text and defers the Latin deviations and glosses to Notes intended for a second part.
editorial_practices.description: The witnesses alternate Latin verse/gloss and English translation, but Bülbring's Part I prints the English as main text and defers the Latin deviations and glosses to Notes intended for a second part.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Uncorrected OCR and TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 encoding
editorial_practices.representation: The item header says no OCR corrections or content editing were performed. Page images are linked to the text.
editorial_practices.description: The item header says no OCR corrections or content editing were performed. Page images are linked to the text.
notes: The closed practice vocabulary has no OCR-production or markup-only normalization term; selection is used solely as the nearest representation-boundary category, while the description records that no verbal normalization is asserted.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete English text with omitted verbal preliminaries
editorial_practices.representation: The XML retains the title page, errata, all 162 target divisions, and in-text apparatus. Many prefatory and residual pages are represented only by PB scaffolding.
editorial_practices.description: The XML retains the title page, errata, all 162 target divisions, and in-text apparatus. Many prefatory and residual pages are represented only by PB scaffolding.

editorial_practices.representation: The repository copy adds NODE to 190 DIV elements and ID to 3,227 NOTE elements, changes EDITORIALDECL N from 1 to 4, and serializes whitespace differently. Canonical normalized bytes match the raw U-M XML exactly after removal of those fields.
editorial_practices.description: The repository copy adds NODE to 190 DIV elements and ID to 3,227 NOTE elements, changes EDITORIALDECL N from 1 to 4, and serializes whitespace differently. Canonical normalized bytes match the raw U-M XML exactly after removal of those fields.
notes: The closed practice vocabulary has no OCR-production or markup-only normalization term; selection is used solely as the nearest representation-boundary category, while the description records that no verbal normalization is asserted.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Pepys-base four-witness edition with Latin and French
editorial_practices.representation: Black and St-Jacques use Pepys 2498 as base, report variants from the other three complete English witnesses, and print the glossed Latin plus all of the French representative in BnF Français 6260.
editorial_practices.description: Black and St-Jacques use Pepys 2498 as base, report variants from the other three complete English witnesses, and print the glossed Latin plus all of the French representative in BnF Français 6260.

open_questions.question: Does the mounted textual fragment on Scheide f.61v/canvas 124 belong to the target work, another text, or later carrier repair?
next_steps: Inspect canvas 124/f.61v with manuscript-catalog expertise and compare its text with the ending of the Athanasian Creed and neighboring works.

open_questions.question: Can TCD provide a complete downloadable MS 69 image set and an exact item-specific license?
next_steps: Ask Digital.Collections@tcd.ie whether MS 69 images exist, whether a complete downloadable 124-leaf object can be supplied, and which exact item license and bulk-download terms apply.

open_questions.question: Can the BL quote a complete expert-photography order for target ff.1r-149v and grant written public-repository redistribution permission?
next_steps: Request a quote for exact target ff.1r–149v and separately request written permission for public Git-repository redistribution; preserve the quote, order confirmation, and permission response.

open_questions.question: Can the Pepys Library supply a complete image set for manuscript pp.263b-370a, and what retention, publication, and redistribution terms would apply?
next_steps: Ask the Pepys Library about complete target pp.263b–370a images, delivery format, price, retention, research use, publication, repository redistribution, and commercial print-on-demand implications.

open_questions.question: Where, if anywhere, does Konrath's working transcript of Psalms 1-37 survive?
next_steps: Search Konrath and Bülbring archival holdings and EETS correspondence for the pre-1890 working transcript; record any shelfmark and access route without assuming survival.

open_questions.question: Can the Latin gloss source or the translator's French physical exemplar be identified more precisely?
next_steps: Compare the Latin glosses and shared French/Middle English readings against securely identified glossed Gallican and French Psalter traditions; keep BnF Français 6260 collateral, not an exemplar.

open_questions.question: Does direct review of the complete 2012 introduction justify a narrower composition date without repeating its unargued precedence claim?
next_steps: Review the complete 2012 Part I introduction, especially pp. xvi and lii, before promoting the 1325–1350 date or any precedence claim.

review_notes: The raw U-M package, its raw XML, the U-M TIFF facsimile, the repository XML, and the IA metadata record are distinct layers even when one ZIP contains several of them.
